english: Mil.; stobs čúṅ-du gró-ba (stobs chung du 'gro ba) the state of declining, the decay of strength Med.; čól-bar gro (chol bar 'gro) they get intermixed, confused Ma.; similarly Tar. 210,10; las zin gró-na (las zin 'gro na) when there is no more work, when work ceases. In a somewhat different sense: mé-tog smán-la gro (me tog sman la 'gro) the blossom is used for medicines. — 5. In W. gró-ba ('gro ba) is gen. joined to a supine in te, and used to express uncertainty or probability: *dí-riṅ der léb-te ḍo* (di ring der leb te do) he has probably arrived there to-day; *zér-te yoḍ ḍo* (zer te yod do) very likely he has said so; *kro ží-te ḍo* (khro zhi te do) his anger will have abated, I think. The origin of this particular use of the word may perh. be traced to such sentences as the following: púṅ-ste gro (phung ste 'gro) we are going to be ruined, we are likely to be ruined. — 6. to be spent, expended, v. gró-sgo ('gro sgo); *sóṅ-to* (song to) col., account of expenses. II. sbst a being, a living creature, gró-ba mi ('gro ba mi) the being 'man', Mil.; gró-ba rin-čén ('gro ba rin chen) Cs., gro-mčóg ('gro mchog), the highest being, or creature, man; gró-bai rigs drúg ('gro ba'i rigs drug), gro-drúg ('gro drug) the six classes of beings, viz. lha (lha), lha-ma-yín (lha ma yin), mi (mi), dúd-gro (dud 'gro), yi-dvágs (yi dwags), dmyál-ba-pa (dmyal ba pa). The lha-ma-yín (lha ma yin) are sometimes omitted, or placed after man. — gro(-bai) don byéd-pa ('gro ba'i don byed pa), or mdzád-pa (mdzad pa) to care for the welfare of beings, which expression is frq. applied to the benevolent activity of the Bodhisatvas etc., at present as much as: to perform divine service, to officiate, = sku-rim byéd-pa (sku rim byed pa). — gro-pán ('gro phan) = gro-dón ('gro don) Tar. 13, 16. — gró-sa ('gro sa) 1. way, road W., *ḍó-sa meṭ* (do sa med) one cannot go there. 2. aim, scope, gró-sar pyín ('gro sar phyin) he reaches his aim, attains his end Glr. 3. access, approach, gró-sa mi dug ('gro sa mi dug) one cannot gain access, admission.

1. (cf. grogs (grogs)) to be associated, kyo-šúg rnyis grógs-na (khyo shug rnyis grogs na) husband and wife together Dzl.; de daṅ grógs-te oṅs (de dang grogs te 'ongs) he came with him, had joined him Dzl.; grógs-te dóṅ-ṅo (grogs te dong ngo) let us go together Dzl.; ṅa daṅ kyéd-rnams bral-méd rtág-tu grogs (nga dang khyed rnams bral med rtag tu grogs) I and you, we shall always remain inseparably united Glr.; grógs-dgos-rnams (grogs dgos rnams) those with whom we are to keep close fellowship, our nearest relations and associates S.g. — 2. cf. grágs-pa (grags pa), sgróg-pa (sgrog pa)) to cry, to shout Dzl., esp. joined with bod ('bod). — 3. (cf. grás-pa (gras pa), grágs-pa (grags pa) I.) to bind, to tie, tág-pa-la dar-lcóg (thag pa la dar lcog), a flag to a rope; to hang, fix, fasten, nyi-mái zér-la (nyi ma'i zer la) hanging on a sun-beam Glr.

1. pf. groṅs (grongs) 1. resp. to die; gróṅs-ka (grongs ka) the very time of one's death Cs., cf. ċí-ka ('chi ka); sometimes it stands 2. for dréṅ-pa ('dreng pa), Mil. and C. — 2. pf. bkróṅs (bkrongs), fut. dgróṅs (dgrongs), resp. to be killed, murdered, put to death, of Lamas and kings.
= bgród-pa (bgrod pa), to go, to travel Glr.

alienism, the state of being a foreigner; grón-du gró-ba (gron du 'gro ba) to go on travels, to go abroad Dzl.; grón-po (gron po), fem. grón-mo (gron mo), guest, foreigner, stranger, traveller frq.; grón-po bód-mkan (gron po 'bod mkhan) one inviting guests, an inviter col.; gzís-gron (gzhis gron) a native guest, byés-gron (byes gron) a foreign guest Cs.; gron-káṅ (gron khang) inn, public house Mil.; gron-gnyér (gron gnyer) 1. that servant in a household who has to announce visitors, to receive and hand over requests etc.; in W. an official in the monasteries attending on strangers and travellers. 2. a mediator, one supporting a petition, one taking care of sacrifices etc. — gron-lám (gron lam) road Cs.

col. *rúm-bu* (rum bu) a small shell, cowry, at present chiefly used as ornament, or as a medicine, after having been burnt and pulverized; gróṅ-bui tal (grong bu'i thal) the ashes of this shell Med.; gron-tód (gron thod) an ornament for the head, consisting of cowries Mil.
I. vb. neut., pf. grol (grol), to become free, to be liberated, released from, bcíṅs-pa gaṅ yín-pa dé-dag ni gról-bar gyúr-to (bcings pa gang yin pa de dag ni grol bar gyur to) all that were bound
